title: Optical Properties of Indium-Gallium-Oxide Microcrystalline Alloy Films: From the Visible to the Deep-UV
authors: HM Borhanul Alam, Dipak Oli, You Qiang, Bisheswor Acharya, Jesse Huso, Matthew D. McCluskey, Leah Bergman
abstract: The tailored optical properties of $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$ microcrystalline films were studied as a function of composition x via transmission, Urbach energy analysis, and spatial photoluminescence (PL) mapping of the self-trapped hole (STH) emission, with the objective of addressing material characteristics specific to this alloy system. Up to x = 0.46, the optical gap exhibited a redshift of 1 eV from the deep to the near-UV range, while the STH PL was redshifted by 0.5 eV in the visible range. For higher composition, x = 0.63, the transmission spectra indicated the co-existence of two optical gaps attributed to Ga-rich and to In-rich domains, implying that this sample is phase-separated. However, the saturation behavior of the optical gap and that of the STH PL showed that incipient phase separation occurs at a lower composition: x ~ 0.3. This is consistent with the compositional trend found for Urbach energy, implying that phase segregation in the alloys is a major defect even at its incipient stages. Additionally, Urbach analysis of $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$ was compared to that of $Mg_xZn_{1-x}O$. Both systems were found to have similar compositional dependence: at lower range, Urbach energies exhibited a negligible increase, while at the higher range a significant dependence on the composition was found. The main difference between the two alloy systems is in their Urbach energy: those for $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$ were significantly larger than those of $Mg_xZn_{1-x}O$. This stems from the strong hole coupling to phonons of $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$, which provides a dynamic transition additionally to that of defect-type.
summary: This paper explores how changing the indium content in (InxGa1-x)2O3 films tunes their optical properties from the deep-UV to the visible spectrum. The researchers discovered that at a composition of x ~ 0.3, the material begins to undergo phase separation, creating defects that significantly impact the optical properties. They also found that the material exhibits much larger Urbach energies than similar alloy systems due to strong coupling between holes and phonons. This work is important for designing transparent conducting oxides and wide-bandgap semiconductors.
main_problem: Investigating the tunable optical properties and the onset of phase separation in (InxGa1-x)2O3 microcrystalline alloy films.
main_result: The study identifies the onset of incipient phase separation at x ~ 0.3, where phase-segregated precipitates act as a major defect type, and finds that large Urbach energies are driven by strong hole-phonon coupling.
method: Wet chemical synthesis followed by UV-Vis transmission spectroscopy, spatial photoluminescence mapping, AFM, and EDS analysis.
model_or_system: Microcrystalline (InxGa1-x)2O3 alloy films ranging from beta-Ga2O3 to In2O3, characterized using a saturation model for the optical gap and an Urbach model for absorption band tails.
key_observables: Optical gap (E_OG), Urbach energy (E_u), self-trapped hole (STH) photoluminescence emission, and compositional-dependent absorption tails.
important_parameters: Indium composition (x), solubility limit/saturation composition (x0 ~ 0.3), and hole-phonon coupling strength.
main_assumptions: The use of a tanh-based saturation model to identify the onset of phase separation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows normalized transmission spectra; Figure 3 and 5 include transmission spectra showing absorption features and the In2O3 optical gap.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the alloy system, describes the synthesis and characterization methods, presents the compositional dependence of the optical gap and STH PL, analyzes phase separation via saturation modeling, compares Urbach energies with the MgxZn1-xO system, and concludes with the impact of hole-phonon coupling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig 1. The normalized transmission spectra for the two end members of the alloy: Ga2O3, and In2O3, as well as for the alloys of composition: x = 0.075, 0.19, 0.31, 0.39, and 0.46. Fig 2. The optical gaps of the samples obtained from the derivative of the transmission spectra.
Figure 3 summarizes the optical gap behavior as a function of alloy composition. Also

Fig 3. The behavior of the optical gap as a function of composition. Also included in the inset is the optical characteristic of a sample with high indium composition x = 0.63 that exhibits two optical gaps weak one at 3.1 eV attributed to In-rich domains and one at the UV range of 4.14 eV due to Ga-rich domains.
Fig 4. The optical gap as a function of composition (diamonds) and a fit of the data using the saturation model of Equation 1 (line) for which a saturation composition x ~ 0.3 was ascertained.

Fig 6. Urbach model plots of the samples of composition x = 0, 0.075, 0.19, 0.39, and 0.46. For each plot, the straight line below the optical gap is a fit to Equation 2 from which the Urbach energy was ascertained.
Fig 5. The transmission spectrum of the In2O3 sample that includes the visible range.
Fig 7. Urbach energies as a function for composition for two alloy systems; MgxZn1-xO (red) and (InxGa1-x)2O3 (blue). The films of these two alloy systems exhibit similar granular morphology.
Fig 8. AFM images for x = 0.19 (top) and x = 0.39 (bottom), showing the granular morphology of the films.
